title: The 'Mormon Wives' Are Headed West. New Spinoff and Cast Announced
summary: Hulu announced a new "Mormon Wives" spinoff called “The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives: Orange County.”
story: There's a new group of Mormon Wives and they're headed west.

During Hulu’s Get Real event on April 22, “Secret Lives of Mormon Wives” stars Miranda Hope, Layla Taylor, Mayci Neeley and Jessi Draper announced that the franchise is expanding with “The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives: Orange County.”

They also announced the new cast, which includes Neeley’s sister, McCall DaPron. Also a part of “Orange County” are Aspyn Ovard, Bobbi Althoff, Avery Woods, Salomé Andrea, Chandler Higginson, Ashleigh Pease and Madison Bontempo.

The new cast expressed their excitement to show audiences a look inside their lives, with one of them saying, “We are thrilled to be part of the franchise, and we are so looking forward to giving you all a little taste of how the West Coast lives.”
The original “Mormon Wives” stars then treated the new cast members with their own dirty soda flavor.
The new installment of “Secret Lives of Mormon Wives” is set to debut on Hulu later this year.

The news comes after filming for Season 5 of “The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives” is resuming, a source with knowledge of the situation told NBC News April 21. Production of the Hulu reality series was put on hold amid domestic violence allegations involving star Taylor Frankie Paul and her ex-boyfriend Dakota Mortensen.
During the Get Real event, McWhorter said that “MomTok will survive,” confirming that the new season is still on its way.

It was not immediately clear if Paul and Mortensen would be involved in the upcoming season. While Mortensen did not immediately respond to a phone call and text message seeking comment, she has responded to Instagram posts suggesting otherwise. Paul could not immediately be reached for comment.

title: Maura Higgins and Ciara Miller to Compete on 'Dancing With the Stars' Season 35
summary: Maura Higgins and Ciara Miller are announced as the first contestants to join Season 35 of "Dancing With the Stars."
story: A brand new bombshell and a “Summer House” star have officially entered the “Dancing With the Stars” ballroom.

During Hulu’s Get Real House event on April 22, “DWTS” hosts Alfonso Ribeiro and Julianne Hough announced that Maura Higgins and Ciara Miller are officially the first celebrities competing for the Len Goodman Mirrorball Trophy for Season 35.

“I’m a strong believer in manifesting. I had it on my vision board,” Higgins said about being part of “DWTS.” “And now it is happening, which I cannot believe, and I know it’s going to be so, so hard. But because I really want to do it, I’m going to put my all into it.”
While she’s “nervous,” she said she's “excited” to compete.
Miller also expressed her excitement. She was not in person but shared via video that she “had some unfinished business” in New York City that she “needed to wrap up.”

“But I am so excited to join Season 35 of ‘Dancing With the Stars.’ I feel like this is the perfect moment. I feel like I’m opening a new chapter, and I’m so excited to share it with all of you guys,” she said. “Can’t wait to put my energy on the dance floor, and Maura I can’t wait to share the dance floor with my fellow faithful. See you soon.”
Expected to return to the judges table are Carrie Ann Inaba, Bruno Tonioli and Derek Hough.

Last season saw record-breaking numbers when it came to viewership and online voting. Robert Irwin and Witney Carson ultimately came out on top, becoming the Season 34 champions. Social media star Alix Earle and Val Chmerkovskiy came in second place.

Irwin was also announced as the host of a “DWTS” spinoff, “Dancing With the Stars: The Next Pro.” The reigning "DWTS" champion and wildlife conservationist along with former dancer Shirley Ballas, who will be a judge alongside son Mark Ballas, introduced the show and shared the first look.

In “The Next Pro,” for the first time in “DWTS” history, 12 dancers will compete for a spot as the next professional dancer on the ABC competition show. They will be joined by a star-studded lineup of returning pros who will serve as rotating mentors/guest judges, according to the press release.
“Dancing with the Stars: The Next Pro” will premiere July 13 on ABC.

title: Patton Oswalt's Wife Responds to Comment That It's 'Tough' to See the Comedian Mourn His Late Spouse
summary: Patton Oswalt’s wife Meredith Salenger responds to comment about the comedian mourning his late wife, Michelle McNamara, on the anniversary of her death.
story: Patton Oswalt’s wife Meredith Salenger isn’t threatened by the love he once shared with his late spouse, Michelle McNamara.
On April 21, Oswalt marked 10 years since McNamara’s death by sharing a tribute on Instagram, writing, “Ten years gone. Miss you, baby.”

In response, Salenger lent her support to her husband, commenting, “What a beauty she was. Smart and cool and brave. Clearly smart enough to love you and wonderful enough to be adored by you. And she made the greatest kid on the planet. Wish I had known her. I’m so sorry for the heartbreak you and Alice will always hold. I love you both with all my heart. Hugs forever. 💖.”
McNamara, an author, died unexpectedly in her sleep in 2016, leaving behind Oswalt and their daughter, Alice. The following year, Oswalt married Salenger, an actor.
Since McNamara's death, Oswalt has honored his late wife on social media on numerous occasions on the anniversary of her passing.
After reading Oswalt's post this week, one Threads user suggested that it must be difficult for his current wife, Salenger, to see him pay tribute to his late wife.
“Always a tough message for a current spouse / partner to read,” they wrote and tagged Salenger.
In response, Salenger made it clear that Oswalt has her full support as he continues to keep his late wife's memory alive.

“Not tough at all. My heart breaks that the two people I love the most lost the person they loved the most. That they ever endured that sadness that will never go away. My heart aches for them both,” she wrote.

Oswalt posted Salenger’s comment along with the following reaction: “Meredith elevates empathy and has guided me and Alice back into the world. This is how she responded to one of my Instagram comments. I found love twice, and I’m thankful every day.”
Many Threads users also lent their support to Oswalt in the comments, including one who shared the following reaction: “Meredith is genuine goodness. I’m so very glad for you.”
Another weighed in, writing, “One of the things I’ve always admired about Meredith is how she speaks about Michelle. Her heart is pure gold.”
Several Threads users noted that anyone who has ever remarried after losing a spouse knows that the heart is capable of loving and  grieving all at once.
“The right people hold our grief as their own and remind us there isn’t a finite amount of love in this world,” one commented.

Another wrote, “It’s baffling to me how many people don’t understand how this works. I’ve known more than one couple where the spouse has no issue with embracing the memory of their spouse’s deceased partner. I have nothing but respect for them, and Meredith.”

title: How the 'Wait for Me' Audiobook Turned the Novel into a Real Musical
summary: Song lyrics and melodies float through "Wait for Me," Read With Jenna's March 2026 book club pick. Here's how the intricate production came to life.
story: Song lyrics and melodies float through "Wait for Me," Read With Jenna's March 2026 book club pick. The audiobook edition makes it possible to actually hear them.

The novel follows two folk singers born decades apart and the ties they have to one another. Marijohn Shaw grows up listening to the music of Elle Harlow, who disappeared at the height of her fame as a folk singer. Their lives connect further as Marijohn pursues her own music career.
Speaking to TODAY.com, Amy Jo Burns says when she was writing the character of Elle, snippets of phrases came to her.
"I don't know what happened here. This isn't a book. It isn't anything," Burns remembers thinking. "Then I realized I was writing song lyrics."

It's one thing to read Elle's lyrics and quite another thing to hear it. The novel's audiobook features full productions of Elle's music, using Burns' lyrics and music composed by Mark Sanderlin, who also performs.
Gail Shalan, an award-winning audiobook narrator, performs as Marijohn, and Patti Murin, who originated the role of Anna in Broadway's "Frozen," performs as Elle.
In a video shared to Macmillan.audio, Burns reacts to hearing one of the songs she wrote come to life.
“I have to pause. It’s just incredible,” she says through tears. “There couldn’t be something better for Elle.”
Macmillan audio producer Ally Demeter, who went to school to become a music producer, was eager to take on the project.
"This was kind of like my love for books and music coming together as one, and being able to make that for the listeners is just honestly a dream come true," she says.
Sanderlin, meanwhile, had "never done anything" as intricate as this project.
"Most of the time, when I've been asked to sing in audio books, it's been either public domain songs or something made up on the fly," he says.

Instead, he was able to compose entire original songs. Getting started, he looked at a playlist Burns made and felt an immediate connection to her influences — Patsy Cline, Johnny Cash and Dolly Parton.

"I got the playlist, and I went, 'Oh, this is all the stuff that I used to listen to in my dad’s truck growing up. I know exactly how to do this.' Taking some of those influences and then applying them to the music was really a fun challenge for me," he says.
While composing, Sanderlin says he would play the demos on walks around New York and fix melodies. Then, he would send the clips to Demeter.
"I would just get goosebumps right away, like, this is incredible. I can't believe this is happening for our audio book," Demeter remembers thinking.

Finally, once the songs were done, Murin, Shalan and Sanderlin spent a day recording over 30 portions of music. Tiny details from the book inspired the music's texture. For example, Murin asked to take the music down a step because "it would fit better with the way Elle's voice is described" in the book, Sanderlin recalls.
Demeter says she "always reads reviews" and has been overwhelmed by the response to the final product, with many saying the music "brings the story to life."
"They just can't believe that this is happening in an audio book. And it just shows that we need to keep pushing forward with the creative medium and just make audiobooks stand out," she says.
But it was Burns’ reaction that affirmed “she did her job right,” Demeter says.

"She was so thankful and appreciative and said, 'It couldn't have been anything more,'" she says. "She was so thankful that we cared so much about the characters in the book and the music in the book."
For now, the "Wait for Me" soundtrack exists only in the audiobook — for listeners, that is.

"I'll still sometimes get the song stuck in my head, because they're just so good," Demeter says. "I have them on my computer, so if I'm ever needing Elle Harlow or Marijohn Shaw, I can just click their song and listen."

title: Henry Winkler Has 1 Hilarious Critique for Statue of The Fonz in Wisconsin
summary: Henry Winkler loves the bronze statue of his famous "Happy Days" character, Arthur "The Fonz" Fonzarelli, except for one aspect.
story: Henry Winkler loves the bronze statue of his famous "Happy Days" character, Arthur "The Fonz" Fonzarelli, except for one aspect.
The legendary actor paid his most recent visit to the "Bronze Fonz" statue in Milwaukee on his 80th birthday in October 2025 and came away with a quibble.
"You know what pisses me off? He’s taller," Winkler joked to Sheinelle Jones and Willie Geist on TODAY April 22. "He’s taller than I am. I don’t look him in the eye."
The 5-foot-6 Winkler made Fonzie into one of television's most legendary characters and an enduring symbol of cool during the run of "Happy Days" from 1974 to 1984.

The show about a group of adolescents growing up in the Midwest in the 1950s and '60s was set in Milwaukee, which honored Winkler with the statue by artist Gerald P. Sawyer on the Milwaukee Riverwalk in 2008.

It depicts Fonzie in his classic black leather jacket, white T-shirt and jeans while giving his iconic "ayyyy" two thumbs up gesture. Winkler shared a photo with his doppelgänger to X in 2016, writing, "OH YES I visit when I'm in Milwaukee."
"What a thing in your life," Winkler said on TODAY. "People told me I would never achieve, and I have a statue. I mean, c’mon. And a cool one. A tall one."

The grandfather of seven remains a television and film mainstay. His latest show, "Hazardous History with Henry Winkler," airs at 9 p.m. ET Sunday nights on the History Channel. It tells the story of reckless moments in American life, when it was normal to use risky products like lawn darts and get swept up in crazes like cramming in phone booths and sitting on flagpoles.
Winkler also stars in the crime thriller "Normal" with Bob Odenkirk, which is in theaters now, about a remote Minnesota town concealing a dark secret.

title: This Is the Way to Have It Your Way: Burger King Launches ‘Mandalorian and Grogu’ Menu
summary: Burger King is launching a limited-time menu in honor of the movie “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu" with four new items that are out of this galaxy.
story: Hop into your Millennium Falcon and take a trip to Burger King to celebrate the upcoming movie “Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu.”

Beginning May 4, Burger King will begin offering a limited time menu in honor of the film, which comes out May 22, is directed by Jon Favreau and stars Pedro Pascal (The Mandalorian), Sigourney Weaver (Colonel Ward), Jeremy Allen White (the voice of Rotta the Hutt) and David Acord (the voice of Grogu).
There are four options:

BBQ Bounty Whopper: A quarter-pound burger that comes in a helmet-shaped carton, in honor of the Mandalorian. It’s topped with melty Swiss cheese, crispy pickle chips, bacon, lettuce, tomato and is covered in creamy Bounty BBQ Sauce.Grogu’s Blue Cookie Shake: This treat is soft serve ice cream mixed with blue sugar cookie syrup and topped with Grogu’s favorite snack, blue cookies.Grogu’s Garlic Chicken Fries: Crispy white meat Chicken Fries are seasoned with parmesan and garlic, served in a Grogu-themed carton with garlic dipping sauce on the side.Imperial Cheddar Ranch Tots: Crispy tots are filled with melty cheddar cheese, potatoes and ranch seasoning, served in an Imperial-themed carton.

BBQ Bounty Whopper: A quarter-pound burger that comes in a helmet-shaped carton, in honor of the Mandalorian. It’s topped with melty Swiss cheese, crispy pickle chips, bacon, lettuce, tomato and is covered in creamy Bounty BBQ Sauce.
Grogu’s Blue Cookie Shake: This treat is soft serve ice cream mixed with blue sugar cookie syrup and topped with Grogu’s favorite snack, blue cookies.
Grogu’s Garlic Chicken Fries: Crispy white meat Chicken Fries are seasoned with parmesan and garlic, served in a Grogu-themed carton with garlic dipping sauce on the side.
Imperial Cheddar Ranch Tots: Crispy tots are filled with melty cheddar cheese, potatoes and ranch seasoning, served in an Imperial-themed carton.

In addition, customers can get four collectible cups with the purchase of the Bounty Bundle (a box featuring all four menu items), the BBQ Bounty Whopper Combo or a 12-piece Grogu’s Garlic Chicken Fry Combo.

There will be a kids’ meal, too, with the Mandalorian and Grogu King Jr. Meal, available starting April 28. That will come with either a hamburger or four-piece nugget, apple sauce and kids’ size french fries, milk or apple juice. Each meal will also include a toy.

“‘Star Wars’ has shaped generations of fans, and as we head into the release of ‘Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu,’ we saw an opportunity to bring that excitement straight into our restaurants,” chief marketing officer of Burger King U.S. and Canada Joel Yashinsky said in a statement.

“‘Star Wars’ has always been about bringing generations of fans together. The Burger King ‘Have It Your Way’ spirit makes them the perfect brand to help fans celebrate the upcoming theatrical release of ‘The Mandalorian and Grogu’ because it’s rooted in bringing people together through shared, memorable experiences,” said Jan Coleman, vice president of partnerships and promotions at The Walt Disney Studios.
“Together, we’re giving fans and families a fun and delicious way to connect with the excitement of the galaxy.”

title: 'Secret Lives of Mormon Wives' To Resume Filming for Season 5
summary: Filming for "The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives" will resume after pausing for more than a month due to domestic violence allegations involving Taylor Frankie Paul and Dakota Mortensen.
story: Filming for Season 5 of "The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives" will resume, a source with knowledge of the situation told NBC News April 21, after pausing for more than a month due to domestic violence allegations involving show star Taylor Frankie Paul and ex-boyfriend Dakota Mortensen.

It was not immediately clear if Paul and Mortensen will be part of the upcoming season. Mortensen did not immediately respond to a phone call and text message seeking comment. Paul could not immediately be reached for comment.

“Mormon Wives” cast member Jessi Draper posted a photo of a restaurant table to her Instagram story April 21 with the caption, "MomTok back together hallelujah." She tagged fellow cast members Mayci Neeley and Layla Taylor as well as makeup artist Peyton Warr.
Filming halted amid reports of a domestic violence investigation involving Paul and Mortensen.

“Mormon Wives” cast member Jessi Draper confirmed to Alex Cooper on the March 25 episode of the “Call Her Daddy” podcast that filming for the reality show was paused by production earlier that month after the cast found out Mortensen had filed a police report. She did not specify when he filed the report and what was alleged in it.
The cast later unanimously decided to pause filming, she said.

“We just didn’t want to be filming and say the wrong thing on camera not knowing the full story yet,” Draper said. “We didn’t want to have to process all this information for the first time and then be speaking about it.”
Utah prosecutors announced in April that they are not charging Paul or Mortensen in relation to domestic violence claims from several incidents that occurred between 2024 and 2026.

Paul was arrested after a 2023 altercation between her and Mortensen. Footage from her arrest was featured in Season 1, Episode 1, of “Secret Lives of Mormon Wives.” On March 19, TMZ published a video appearing to show Paul attacking Mortensen and reportedly from that February 2023 incident. NBC News has not independently verified the circumstances and authenticity of the video.

That same year Paul was charged with assault, criminal mischief and domestic violence in the presence of a child. She pleaded guilty in abeyance to an aggravated assault charge in August 2023, and the other charges were dropped, according to court records.
In the wake of the video becoming public, Disney canceled Paul's season of “The Bachelorette,” which was supposed to premiere on March 22.

Paul and Mortensen share a son, Ever, 2, who has been caught up in the legal proceedings. Mortensen filed a temporary protective order against Paul on March 20, prompting her to lose custody of Ever until the pair met in court on April 7.

Just an hour before the hearing on April 7, Paul filed and was later granted a temporary protective order against Mortensen with a declaration attached in which she alleged years of abuse at the hands of Mortensen.

A judge ruled Paul could have up to eight hours of supervised time with Ever per week until they meet in court again on April 30 to determine the “merits and entry” of both Paul’s and Mortensen’s protective orders.

title: Where Is Michael Barisone Now? The True Story of 'Untold: The Shooting at Hawthorne Hill"
summary: Who is Michael Barisone? What did he do to his student Lauren Kanarek? The true story of the Shooting at Hawthorne Hill.
story: An equestrian dressage trainer who was acquitted on charges of attempted murder and the former trainee he shot are each sharing their sides of a story that rocked the elite equestrian community 2019.

The conflict between Olympian and dressage coach Michael Barisone and equestrian rider Lauren Kanarek is the subject of Netflix’s “Untold: The Shooting at Hawthorne Hill,” which was released on April 21.
Here is what to know about the case.
Who Is Michael Barisone?
Barisone, 62, was an alternate on the 2008 Olympic team in the equestrian sport of dressage who went on to become a trainer and coach in the sport.
Dressage involves riders guiding horses through a set of planned movements while a panel of judges rates them.
Outside of the Olympics, Barisone won over 100 CDI Grand Prix events, including a World Cup gold medal in 1997, according to the U.S. Eventing Association.
In 2012, he tutored Stephen Colbert in dressage ahead of the London Olympics for a comic segment that aired on Comedy Central’s “The Colbert Report.”
After his competition career, he began training aspiring dressage riders, splitting time between farms in New Jersey and Florida.
“At the end of the day, if someone has a problem and I can help them get past it and succeed, that makes me happy,” Barisone told the U.S. Eventing Association in 2018.
Why Did Michael Barisone Shoot Lauren Kanarek?
Kanarek and Barisone first met in 2018, and he invited her to train in dressage at his farm in Long Valley, N.J.,Barisonesays in the Netflix documentary.
Kanarek and her fiancé, Rob Goodwin, per NJ.com, moved to Hawthorne Hill, the name of Barisone‘s farm, to train under him.

However, their relationship soured in 2019, resulting in Barisone having his attorney draft an eviction notice as he tried to get them to leave his property, according to The Daily Record of Morristown, N.J.

Barisone says in the Netflix documentary that Kanarek commandeered one of his horses for her own use at dressage shows in July 2018. He said when he told her and Goodwin that they could no longer show the horse, the couple bullied and threatened him into selling them the horse for a low price.
“Getting bullied into buying a horse is the biggest piece of bulls--- I’ve ever heard in my entire life, period,” Kanarek replies in the Netflix doc.

Barisone’s attorney, Edward Bilinkas, who appears in the Netflix documentary, argued in court in 2022 that Barisone believed Kanarek was out to destroy his life and business, according to The Daily Record. In “Untold,”Barisone said Kanarek made disparaging Facebook posts about him in which she wrote she was ready to “go to war” with him. He also holds up a printout of a lengthy Facebook post by Kanarek.
Kanarek says in “Untold” that she made the posts because Barisone‘s girlfriend at the time was preventing her from riding at the farm.
In a 911 call in July 2019, Barisone said Goodwin and Kanarek were “menaces to society,” according to a recording of the call in “Untold.”
“This is a war, and it’s going to be dealt with right now,” he says in the phone call.
The escalating tensions then led to a shocking moment.
On Aug. 7, 2019, Barisone took a gun out of his safe and drove down to the house where Kanarek and Goodwin were staying on the farm. He then shot Kanarek twice in the chest, per The Daily Record.
“I’m walking over to him, and he is just staring at me,” Kanarek says in the documentary. “There was just something not right. And immediately, Michael pulls out a gun, shoots at me, bang, bang.”

She called 911 and said she had been shot in the chest, according to the recording aired in “Untold.” Kanarek survived after being placed in a medically induced coma and having surgery to repair her left lung, according to The Associated Press.
What Happened in the Court Case Between Barisone and Kanarek?
Morris County prosecutors charged Barisone with two counts of first-degree attempted murder and two counts of possession of a weapon for an unlawful purpose, according to The Daily Record.
He was charged with two counts of first-degree attempted murder because police said he also fired at Goodwin, missing him.

During the trial in 2022, Bilinkas argued that Barisone acted out of insanity and self-defense, saying in “Untold” that Barisone was in a “delusional state” at the time of the shooting. He told the jury that his client had been “mentally tortured” by Kanarek and Goodwin and had been struggling with anxiety, delusions and suicidal thoughts, according to The Daily Record.
“Every man has his breaking point,” Bilinkas said in his opening statement, according to video from the Netflix documentary.

Morris County Chief Assistant Prosecutor Chris Schellhorn countered by telling the jury in his opening statement that even though they may hear things that they “don’t like” about Kanarek, it didn’t warrant being shot and nearly killed, according to video of his opening argument in “Untold” and recorded in the equestrian magazine The Chronicle of the Horse. He also argued that Barisone retrieving the gun from the safe and loading it indicated the act was premeditated.

“We’re here on this trial because of the defendant’s choice on Aug. 7, 2019,” Schellhorn told the jury. “His choice to get a gun, to point that gun at Lauren and pull the trigger twice, and then to turn it on Rob and pull the trigger again.”

Two doctors called to the stand by Bilinkas testified that Barisone had a delusional disorder that left him unaccountable for his actions when he shot Kanarek. A doctor called by the prosecution said there was no basis to conclude Barisone had any type of delusional disorder and that his thoughts were “reality based” at the time of the shooting. according to The Daily Record.
Barisone did not testify at the trial.

On April 14, 2022, the jury found Barisone not guilty by reason of insanity for the charges of attempted murder against Kanasek and Goodwin, according to The Associated Press. He also was acquitted on the two weapons counts. He had faced up to 60 years in prison if he was convicted.
The Morris County Prosecutor’s Office issued a statement after the verdict, saying it was “disappointed with the outcome,” but “the verdict must be respected.”
Where Is Michael Barisone Now?
Barisone, who spent two and a half years years in prison until his acquittal, was civilly committed for evaluation at the Ann Klein Forensic Center in Trenton for 30 days in the wake of the verdict.

He then was sent to the Greystone Park Psychiatric Hospital in Morris Plains, N.J., where he faced Krol hearings to determine whether he was still a danger to himself or the public due to mental illness, according to the Morris County Prosecutor’s Office 2023 Annual Report.
Bilinkas says in "Untold" that doctors did "extensive" tests on Barisone and determined he was not a threat to the community, which allowed him to be released from Greystone in 2023.
"I've been given a chance to have a new life, and I want to live it well," Barisone says in the documentary.
“I do not think Michael Barisone is insane one single bit,” Kanarek counters.
Barisone says his goal is now to make it back to the Olympics or another Gold Cup final in dressage.

However, he was permanently banned in 2025 by Safesport, which prevents him from riding in events sanctioned by the U.S. Equestrian Foundation. The organization cited the reason as sexual harassment; emotional misconduct; violated NGB policies/bylaws, according to its website. The decision is subject to appeal, according to Safesport.

title: KATSEYE Concert on TODAY: What You Need to Know
summary: On Friday, Aug. 14, KATSEYE are performing LIVE on TODAY as part of the Citi Concert Series! Find out how to register for a Fan Pass and more.
story: On Friday, Aug. 14, KATSEYE are performing LIVE in New York City as part of the Citi Concert Series on TODAY, and you could be there! Here’s everything you need to know to attend the concert:
Date: Friday, Aug. 14
Check-in: by 5:15 a.m. (concert concludes at 9:30 a.m.)

title: George W. Bush Has a Theory for Why People Love His Friendship with Michelle Obama
summary: George W. Bush has a theory for why people love his relationship with Michelle Obama. He told Jenna Bush Hager in an interview.
story: George W. Bush didn't initially understand all the hoopla surrounding his friendship with former first lady Michelle Obama.
But he later figured out how novel it was for some folks to see two people from different backgrounds “having fun” together.

When the four living former presidents recently spoke to Jenna Bush Hager for a TODAY exclusive about about the nation’s upcoming 250th anniversary, George W. Bush — Jenna's dad — reflected on how a cute moment he and Michelle Obama shared at John McCain's September 2018 funeral went viral.

"I get a little antsy, as I’m sure you know. And I was sitting next to Michelle, that’s who I sit next to at funerals," joked President Bush, referencing the political protocol that seats him next to the former first lady at official events.
"I was kinda teasing her and stuff, and I slipped her an Altoid. Not as a joke, but  I thought she might want one," he continued, laughing.
"And I got in the car afterwards and you said, 'You’re trending," he told Jenna, still laughing. "I didn’t know what trending meant."

"It turns out the country is starved to see," he said, "a white center right Republican and an African-American center left Democrat having fun, and being able to converse, not as political figures, but as citizens."
In October 2018, Michelle Obama opened up during an interview on TODAY about the pair's "cough drop" moment.
“President Bush and I, we are forever seatmates because of protocol, and that’s how we sit at all the official functions,” she said.
“He’s my partner-in-crime at every major thing where all the ‘formers’ gather. So we’re together all the time.”
“I love him to death,” she added. “He’s a wonderful man, he’s a funny man.”
President Bush and the former first lady previously made headlines for sharing a warm embrace at the National Museum of African American History and Culture dedication ceremony in 2016.
When he appeared on Ellen DeGeneres' talk show in 2017, President Bush talked about his special bond with the former first lady.
“(The friendship) surprised everybody,” he said. “That’s what’s so weird about society today, (the surprise) that people on opposite sides f the political spectrum can actually like each other.”

The two pals continued their sweet tradition in December 2018 when President Bush offered the former first lady a mint or cough  drop while greeting President Trump and the former presidents and first ladies at the funeral for his father, former president George H.W. Bush.
The former president leaned in front of President Trump in the church's front pew to offer the mint to Michelle Obama, causing her husband, former president Barack Obama, to laugh.
The former first lady discussed the cute moment while on tour for her memoir, “Becoming.”
“He has the presence of mind and the sense of humor to bring me a mint, and he made it a point to give me that mint right then and there, and that’s the beauty of George Bush,” she said.

The following year, the former first lady opened up to Jenna about her friendship with President Bush during their trip to Vietnam to raise awareness about the importance of education for young girls.
“I had the opportunity to sit by your father at funerals, the highs and the lows, and we shared stories about our kids and about our parents,” she told Jenna.
“Our values are the same. We disagree on policy, but we don’t disagree on humanity. We don’t disagree about love and compassion.”
“I think that’s true for all of us. It’s just that we get lost in our fear of what’s different,” she added.

title: Donny Osmond Calls Older Brother Alan ‘My Protector’ In Tribute After His Death at 76
summary: Donny Osmond is mourning the death of his older brother Alan with a post on Facebook, after Alan Osmond died at the age of 76.
story: Donny Osmond is mourning the death of his older brother Alan.
Alan Osmond died at the age of 76, his family shared. A cause of death was not provided.

“This is one of the earliest pictures I have of my brother Alan and me,” Donny Osmond captioned a throwback black and white photo of them April 21 on social media. The shot featured Alan Osmond as a child hugging Donny Osmond as a toddler.

“Even back then, you can see that he had his arm around me, watching over me. That’s who he was. My protector. My guide. The one who quietly carried so much responsibility so the rest of us could shine," he wrote.

Donny Osmond credited his big brother for helping their family achieve success. The Osmonds performed as a family musical troupe in the 1970s, beginning with Alan, Wayne, Merrill and Jay Osmond, before Donny and Jimmy Osmond joined. Marie Osmond, their only sister, later teamed up with Donny for the "Donny & Marie” variety show on ABC.

“Alan was our leader in every sense of the word. His tireless work helped build everything we became. I will always be grateful for the sacrifices he made and the love he showed—not just to me, but to every member of our family,” Donny Osmond wrote.
“I owe him more than I can ever fully express. I love you, Alan. Thank you for always being there for me. Till we meet again, Brother Donny," he concluded.
Alan Osmond was the third born of the Osmonds, but the oldest of those who would go on to perform. He was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis in 1987,  per KSL, an NBC affiliate in Salt Lake City.
“Our mother always said, ‘Positive mental attitude,’ so I said, ‘I may have MS, but MS does not have me,'" Alan Osmond said in 2023.
Merrill Osmond, 72, who also performed with his siblings, mourned his older brother's death in a statement on Facebook.
“His life was not measured in years, but in love, sacrifice, and purpose,” he wrote, in part, on Facebook.

“And I need you to know this… he has not left me. I have felt him. I have felt his quiet encouragement telling me to keep going… to keep building faith… to keep sharing light. His testimony is not gone, it lives on, and it will continue to be felt far beyond this life,” he added.
“So please, don’t let your hearts be heavy," he continued. "Don’t weep for him. Rejoice, knowing that your brother, your friend, your hero is no longer in pain. He is free. He is whole. He is home.”

title: Stacy London and Clinton Kelly Say Their New Show Is a Mix of' Snark' and 'Entertainment'
summary: Stacy London and Clinton Kelly dish on their newest passion: movie fashion. The long-time friends unpack their favorite films and the outfits that defined them.
story: Stacy London and Clinton Kelly are settling in with a bucket of popcorn and they’re hoping you’ll join them as they ponder some of film’s most iconic styles in the new podcast and web series “Why’d They Wear That?”

Known for the TLC show “What Not to Wear,” which aired from 2003 to 2013, along with its recent follow-up “Wear Whatever the F You Want,” the long-time friends are unpacking their considerable encyclopedia of film and fashion knowledge and using it to watch memorable movies and TV shows with a focus on style.

“What is ‘pop culture’ really?’ asks Kelly. “Pop culture is the movies you watch, the television shows you watch, the music you listen to, the videos you watch, the food you eat ... so it’s all this great interconnected web.”

That symbiotic relationship between film and fashion is the focus of “Why’d They Wear That?” and according to the duo, it’s an effective way to marry their opinions on style with their mutual love of cinema.

“We bonded over a lot of movies, so we wanted to start with some of those,” London says of the classics they chose to cover, including Steven Spielberg’s 1982 blockbuster “Poltergeist” and the beloved romcom “When Harry Met Sally,” among others.
Though scrutinizing appearances is familiar territory for London and Kelly, they’re quick to say that the new series is about examining movie fashion rather than criticizing it.

“It’s not really a take-down and critique of style as much as it is really talking about how style affected us,” explains London of the new series, which is a marked departure from the sometimes-stinging observations they once deployed in “What Not to Wear.”
“We got tired of being told that we were too mean. Then we did ‘Wear Whatever the F You Want,’ and people said we were too nice,” London says.

“Now, we’re just like, ‘What if we’re just ourselves? What if we can be a little bit snarky but instead of about real people, we’ll put in a fictionalized way so that you can get the snark and entertainment without the punch of being critical about someone?” she explains.
Kelly agrees and adds that unlike their previous shows, which he says were “very formatted,” the new series is just the opposite and affords them the luxury of simply being themselves.

“In real life, we just don’t shut up. We just build on each other’s jokes and get a kick out of each other. We still, after 25 years, make each laugh, which is crazy,” Kelly says. “So, it’s just us being us.”

But, as fans might expect, they still have plenty of thoughts about what some of film’s most memorable characters are wearing, including Diane Keaton’s enduring style in “Annie Hall,” which London says “created a fashion style on its own” and Michelle Pfeiffer’s turn in “Scarface,” who Kelly says is “amazing in every single thing” she wears.

Other movie looks they mention include Gwyneth Paltrow’s style in “Great Expectations,” with London pointing out that Paltrow only wears green throughout the movie, as well as Melanie Griffiths’ looks in the 1988 classic “Working Girl,” including her big hair and statement makeup.

“It’s so interesting to see the use of color, especially in ‘80s movies, because color is really coming back into fashion again,” London says, adding, “You see where things started and you see how things have been reinterpreted.

London and Kelly are less enthusiastic about the fashion in “When Harry Met Sally,” with London calling it “dated” by today’s standards and saying, “Who told (Meg Ryan) that hat was a good idea?” Clinton says the rom-com’s wardrobe is “so boring.”

That said, Kelly clarifies that their commentary has nothing to do with the actors or the costume designers. “It’s just us saying, ‘How does this outfit make you feel in the context of the movie and in the context of the world then, and in the context of the world now,” he says.
Either way, they say, movie fashion can become a cultural touchstone. And whether you love or hate it, chances are pretty good that you’re somehow influenced by it.

“Even if you’re not watching a movie with an eye toward the style, you still absorb something from it,” Kelly says. “It affects you whether you realize it or not. Like when you identify with a character, you might not even notice what that character is wearing, but you’re like, ‘Oh, there’s something about that person that makes me want to be like them,’” he explains.

“I think Clinton’s right,” London says, “especially when we’re talking about it in terms of culture, in terms of entertainment. You don’t know how much you’re absorbing. So, it may be that you see green in ‘Great Expectations’ and you go out and you buy green velvet.”
All fashion aside, London and Kelly say that ultimately “Why’d They Wear That?” is about having a good time.
“We want people to have a laugh,” London says. “I mean, more than anything, a laugh. The world needs a laugh.”

title: Abbey Romeo's Mom Shares What Led to Breakup with David from ‘Love on the Spectrum’
summary: The mother of “Love on the Spectrum” star Abbey reacts to her daughter's breakup with David.
story: Longtime “Love on the Spectrum” stars Abbey Romeo and David Isaacman have broken up after nearly five years together, Abbey's mom, Christine Romeo, confirmed in a video after news of their split broke just over a week after Season 4 of the reality dating show hit Netflix.

"David was happy in his way of doing life and Abbey's was different," Christine said in a YouTube video posted April 18 alongside Abbey’s cousin Mary Romeo. "And they found that out over this period of time."
Abbey and David met on Season 1 of “Love on the Spectrum.” They starred on the Netflix series until Season 3 and became recurring members in Season 4.
Mary, who helps as Abbey's aide and often facilitated her dates with David, said the couple's "No. 1 connection" was their special interests.

"That sparked so much," Mary said. "But then I think over time, when you're discovering more about your growth and what you want out of life and their personal journeys and stuff, I think that they just kind of ended up growing in different directions."
"Their love of lions is so pure, but I don't think it was maybe enough for what they needed in their next step of the relationship," Mary added.
Christine also cleared up rumors that their split stemmed from a fight.

“I feel that everyone’s kind of missing the point on Abbey and David,” she said. “I think they will have a forever bond. There was no fight, by the way ... Just so we can be clear on that. There was no fight over engagements and rings being thrown around.”
Christine said she's "grateful" for the time Abbey and David had together and credits their relationship for helping her daughter grow as a person.

“Abbey has grown, a ton since she started both social media and ‘Love on the Spectrum,’” Christine said. “She started to understand her own story, see herself as a person with autism in the world, learn to accept her autism and still try to have a relationship and fit in and all of these things."
“None of the growth would have happened without David. That’s what relationships are. They help us grow and change, and sometimes it’s perfect, and people stay together,” she added.
She said David acted as a "mirror" for Abbey "to see and figure out what she wants" and "who she is."
Christine also reminded people not compare her daughter’s relationship with David to neurotypical dynamics.

“Please don’t judge autistic relationships as if they were typical,” she said. “So many people on the spectrum are not going to do what typical people do. They’re not in it to get married, get a house, get a new car, have children. They may never live together as a married couple.”
Mary pointed out that for anyone, a five-year relationship can be a success story.

“I think, for anybody, a five-year relationship is a successful relationship," Mary said. "You learn so much, you grow so much, and you take that to your next relationship, and I think that it obviously was a success."

title: Madonna Offering Reward for Personal Vintage Pieces Missing After Coachella Performance
summary: Madonna reveals that vintage costumes from her personal college have gone missing after her Coachella performance with Sabrina Carpenter.
story: Madonna is offering a reward after some of her vintage costumes recently went missing.

The music icon surprised the Coachella audience on April 17 when she joined Sabrina Carpenter on stage for week two of the music festival. Madonna appeared in an all-lavender look, featuring a corset bodysuit, leather jacket, gloves and tights.
But after enjoying her fun weekend in the California desert, the Grammy winner announced on her Instagram story that multiple archival costumes, including the pieces she wore at Coachella, are gone.

“Still flying high since Friday night at Coachella!” she began in a statement posted April 20. “Thank you to Sabrina and everyone who made it possible. Bringing Confessions II back to where it began was such a thrill!”

She continued, “This full circle moment hit different until I discovered that the vintage pieces that I wore went missing — my costume that was pulled from my personal archives — jacket, corset, dress and all other garments.”
Madonna explained that the missing items hold special significance.
“These aren’t just clothes, they are part of my history. Other archival items from the same era went missing as well,” she added.
The “Like a Prayer” singer encouraged anyone with knowledge about the costumes to contact her team at infomaverick2026@gmail.com.
She also confirmed that she is offering a reward for whoever safely returns the clothing.
Madonna added pictures of the costumes, including an all-pink ensemble, to her Instagram story, too.
She later uploaded multiple behind-the-scenes pictures from her Coachella performance with Carpenter to Instagram.
“A moment in history, I will never forget!” she said, in part, in the caption. “I FEEL SO FREE.”

“I Feel So Free” is the title of her latest single from her upcoming album, “Confessions on a Dance Floor Part II,” which will arrive July 3. Madonna revealed the new album is on the way on April 15, ahead of her Coachella moment.
Her original “Confessions of a Dance Floor” album, her tenth record, came out in November 2005. It included hits like “Hung Up” and “Jump.”

Carpenter thanked Madonna on Instagram April 18 for joining her onstage. The “Espresso” singer shared a carousel of photos from her week two show and wrote in the caption, in part, “Madonna ..…I’ve got something i wanna talk about!”

She added, “Thank you for coming out, bringing your love, and gracing the audience with everything you are + astrology knowledge + the greatest songs of all time. Last night was straight out of a dream. spending so much time laughing with you and then above all sharing the stage with you is a privilege I’ll never forget.”

title: Christina Applegate Breaks Silence After Reports of Hospitalization
summary: Christina Applegate shared an update on her health amid reports that she was recently hospitalized.
story: Christina Applegate is recovering following reports of her being hospitalized.
The 54-year-old actor, who was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis in June 2021, shared a statement on Instagram amid her ongoing health struggles.
“Thank you for the outpouring of love and well wishes,” she wrote April 20. “Health issues are a constant for me, but I’m a strong chick and I’m getting stronger and better every day.”
She added, “I’m taking a moment to focus on my health, but I’ll be back with more to say soon enough.”

The post included a photo of a mug that read “Kissy Kissy” and her recently published memoir, “You With the Sad Eyes,” which details her battle with MS and other challenges she has overcome during her career.
Celebrity friends left supportive messages in the comments.
Actor Cheyenne Jackson said, “Christina, just in case today’s mail got lost: We the public would like to formally submit that we adore you, support you, and RIDE AT DAWN FOR YOU ALWAYS.”
“Days of Our Lives” star Greg Rikaart wrote, “Can’t think of anyone the collective culture roots for more than you.”
Olivia Munn simply left multiple red hearts in the comments.
Applegate’s health update comes after TMZ first reported on April 16 that the “Married with Children” star was taken to the hospital in late March.

Her rep told NBC News in a statement April 16, “I have no comment on whether she is in the hospital or what her medical treatments are. She’s had a long history of complicated medical conditions that she has been refreshingly open about, as evidenced in her memoir and on her podcast.”
Applegate publicly revealed she was diagnosed with MS in August 2021 while working on her Netflix series “Dead To Me.”

“Hi friends. A few months ago I was diagnosed with MS. It’s been a strange journey,” she wrote on X at the time. “But I have been so supported by people that I know who also have this condition. It’s been a tough road. But as we all know, the road keeps going. Unless some asshole blocks it.”
Since then, she has repeatedly spoken about how the disease, which disrupts communication between the brain and rest of the body according to Mayo Clinic, affects her day-to-day life.
In “You With the Sad Eyes,” Applegate said that she has to go to the emergency room frequently.

“When I wake up, I often can’t get my arm to move far enough to grab the cup of water by my bed or my phone from its charger,” she said, before adding that she is prone to infection and the disease affects her stomach, too.
“I start vomiting, almost like my body is trying to stop the noise, too,” Applegate wrote. “Then I end up in the hospital again. I just want everything to stop. But it won’t stop.”
While speaking about the memoir with the Los Angeles Times, the actor said it is important that those with MS share their experiences.

“We need a community. We need a bunch of us talking to each other — caretakers and children of people with MS and all this stuff. I just want to have a kumbaya with everybody right now because there’s so much sadness with this disease,” she explained.
Applegate also co-hosts a podcast called “MeSsy” with Jamie-Lynn Sigler, who has also been diagnosed with MS.
During a March 2025 episode of the podcast, Applegate confirmed that since her diagnosis she has “been in the hospital upwards of 30 times.”

title: It’s a Sunshine Day for ‘Brady Bunch’ Fans! The Two Marcia Bradys Just Met Up
summary: Maureen McCormick and Christine Taylor, the two actors who have played Marcia in “The Brady Bunch” universe, met up.
story: “Marcia, Marcia, Marcia!” has become “Marcia, Marcia!”
The two actors who have played the oldest daughter in “The Brady Bunch” universe hooked up to take a selfie.

“Marcia! Marcia! Marcia’s! It’s a Sunshine Day 🎶🌼🌈☀️💖🦋 ,” Maureen McCormick captioned a photo of her and Christine Taylor April 19 on Instagram while alluding to “Sunshine Day,” the song the Brady kids sang in a 1973 Season 4 episode while appearing on an amateur hour TV show.
“My heart is so full! I love and adore you @stillceej💓😘xoxo”
Taylor was equally delighted to hang with McComick.
“A far too long overdue meeting of the Marcias! 👩‍❤️‍💋‍👩🌻👭🌞🏈💞 It was pure joy, my fellow Leo! I love you dearly, and until the next time, we’re gonna Keep On Groovin’ 🎶😂😉” she wrote in the comments.

McCormick, of course, originated the role of popular teenager Marcia on “The Brady Bunch” series, which aired for five seasons from 1969 until 1974. Taylor played the character in the 1995 film “The Brady Bunch Movie,” as well as 1996’s “A Very Brady Sequel.”

“My favorite memories of filming, I think, were when we weren’t filming, and we were just hanging out on the set as friends and spending weekends together. It was like a second extended family,” McCormick told TODAY in 2019 about her time with the series.
As for the show’s enduring popularity across multiple generations, McCormick said people are “looking for a place to escape and just to go back to simpler, simpler times.

“And I think there’s basic things in life that are always really important and never change, and those are certain values. And I think the values in our show were really, really good, about sticking together and working things out as a family, and helping each other.”
Taylor, meanwhile, says Marcia is the role fans know her from the most.

“The crazy thing — and I remember even when we were doing press for the movie and the sequel — people always thought that I was the original Marcia, Maureen McCormick,” she told Entertainment Weekly in 2015.

“The plumber would come to the house and be like, ‘Oh, I grew up watching your show!’ You’ve just gotta go with it. There’s no need to clarify at that point, because they’ve got the right character, I don’t need to say, ‘No, I was the one in the movie.’ I just let people enjoy whichever version of Marcia they think I am.”
Taylor also said she was “obsessed” with “The Brady Bunch” growing up and people had told her she resembled McCormick.

“I did hear that I looked like her growing up, so I always had this fascination with the show and had lines memorized. I would do impressions of Marcia for my friends even before I became an actress,” she said.

title: Olympian Noah Lyles Got Backlash for How He Reacted to His Wife Junelle Bromfield's Wedding Dress. She Just Responded
summary: Olympic sprinter Junelle Bromfield Lyles responded after his reaction to her wedding dress goes viral.
story: Olympic sprinter Junelle Bromfield Lyles is setting the record straight after her husband, U.S. Olympic track star Noah Lyles, received criticism for his reaction to her wedding dress in a first-look video taken on the day of their nuptials.

A Tiktok video posted by Stanlo Photography on April 14 shows Lyles, 28, with his back turned as Bromfield approaches in her wedding dress. She taps him on the shoulder and he turns the wrong way at first before spinning around to admire her look.
"Oh wow, okay. I didn’t think you would go with a princess dress," Lyles says. "I didn’t. Yeah, this is good. Dang, that’s a train — and we’re supposed to spin in this?”
The reigning Olympic 100-meter champion drew criticism from people who said his response to his bride's look was lackluster.
"He didn't even compliment her ONCE," one commenter wrote.
"This wouldn't even be an acceptable reaction from a friend," another commented.
"My dog would be more excited to see me," another wrote.
Bromfield, 28, an Olympic sprinter from Jamaica, took to TikTok to give some background on the moment and respond to the critics.

"So if it takes somebody saying you’re not beautiful to call off an engagement or a marriage, it wasn’t worth anything," she said. "Because truth be told, that is something that I get told every day by Noah. So in that moment, that dress, that hair, that makeup, did not matter. All I wanted was to feel comfortable. All I wanted was to laugh, to smile, and he did exactly that.”
Bromfield also shared some background about her emotional state on their wedding day.

The Jamaican sprinter said that all of her sisters, her best friend and other family members were denied visas to come to their wedding on April 4 at The Conservatory at Blackberry Ridge in Trenton, Georgia.
"It was just a lot emotionally going in because I wanted my family to be there," she said.
She then learned that the person who was supposed to deliver the first speech at the reception and sit in the front did not show up.

"So Noah was told like an hour before, you know, somebody did not show up, and we didn't tell Junelle as yet," she said. "He said, 'Tell her now, so she can get to process it.' They were like, 'No, we're going to tell her before the first look, so when she sees you, she can cheer up, like lift her spirit.'"

"So five minutes before walking out that door, I was told that this person did not show up," she continued. "Went out there basically holding back tears or whatever. And there was like 10 cameras around us. When I looked to the side, my aunt was halfway passing out, so she was sitting outside just catching some air. And I'm there overstimulated, just tapping Noah on the shoulder."
Bromfield took issue with the notion that Lyles' response wasn't appropriate or enthusiastic enough.

"So in that moment, that reaction that I got from Noah is exactly what I needed because he tried to make me happy," she said. "He tried to make me talk because if he did not do that I would probably be, what do we call it now, shut off that whole wedding because I was processing so much disappointment mentally and emotionally. So what he gave, I truly appreciate."
The bride also explained her husband's reaction to that particular dress. She said she had three dresses ready for the wedding day — a mermaid dress, a princess dress and a short dress to dance in.
She initially told Lyles she was going to wear the mermaid dress, which she knew he "hates," but decided to go with the princess dress instead.

"He was very surprised that I was in a princess dress," she said. "So he liked it. And this is something that we talked about. We talked about how I would dance in that princess dress if I actually wore it."
Bromfield said most of the negative reactions to the first look video came from other women.

"This is the first time I've seen so many women giving so many opinions on my relationship," she said. "Some are saying, 'Run. He didn't say you're beautiful.' And every time I read those comments ... I look at these profiles, and it's usually a woman that looks like me, or, 'He don't love her, why can't he love me?' I am beautiful, I am strong, I’m ambitious and I’m confident.'"

The couple started dating in 2022 and announced their engagement in 2024. They shared the news about two months after Lyles won gold in the men’s 100-meter race and bronze in the men’s 200-meter race at the 2024 Paris Olympics.
Bromfield has had past Olympic success as a member of the bronze medal-winning Jamaican women's 4x400 team at the 2020 Tokyou Olympics.

title: Dakota Mortensen Returns to Social Media After 'Closing an Unhealthy Chapter’
summary: Dakota Mortensen broke a monthlong hiatus from Instagram with a post on April 20 amid his controversy with ex-girlfriend Taylor Frankie Paul.
story: “The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives” star Dakota Mortensen broke a monthlong hiatus from Instagram with a post on April 20.
The post touches on what he calls “a difficult cycle” and the toll it took on Ever, the son he shares with Taylor Frankie Paul.
While not explicitly mentioning Paul, the post refers to “chaos” that unfolded recently.

“Over the past several weeks, I’ve taken a step back and had time away from the chaos,” Mortensen wrote in a post with comments disabled. “That space has been a breath of fresh air and has given me clarity. My focus now is where it should have been all along — on my son and creating a stable, healthy environment for him moving forward."
“I regret not stepping away from a difficult cycle sooner. I’m not perfect, and I take accountability for my part. I’m sincerely sorry to those I’ve hurt.

“At the same time, I don’t agree with how this situation has been portrayed publicly. It does not reflect the full context of what occurred or the impact it has had on me and my son,” Mortensen continued.

“I’ve chosen to step away from the spotlight to focus on my family, my growth, and our healing, and that’s where my energy will remain. I’m closing an unhealthy chapter and moving forward with hope for better days ahead.”
Mortensen, whose last Instagram post before this one was on March 15, thanked those people who’ve stood by his side and reiterated his intention to take care of his son.

“I’m grateful for all of the encouragement and support I’ve received, and I’m committed to showing up in a way that reflects that,” he wrote. “My intention, over time and when appropriate, is to use what I’ve learned in a way that supports others navigating similar situations.
“For now, my priority is on showing up as the best father I can be for Ever,” he ended his post, while adding a link to a number for the National Domestic Violence Hotline.

Mortensen’s tumultuous relationship with Paul took the stage in the Hulu show “The Secret Lives of Mormon Wives,” and has made headlines recently. In the lead-up to Paul’s season of “The Bachelorette,” TMZ published a video from 2023 that showed Paul attacking and throwing chairs at Mortensen. Her season of “The Bachelorette” was cancelled by ABC amid the fallout.

Paul was arrested in 2023 and charged with three misdemeanors including assault and committing domestic violence when a child was present. She reached a plea deal in her assault case in late August 2023. She pleaded guilty to aggravated assault, and the other charges against her were dismissed with prejudice, meaning they were permanently dismissed, according to court documents viewed by The Salt Lake Tribune.
The couple has also faced more recent charges of domestic violence. In February of this year, Mortensen filed a domestic violence complaint against Paul and Paul counter-claimed an assault by him.
Utah prosecutors declined to file charges against Paul in April 2026, citing a lack of evidence. Two days later, prosecutors also declined to file charges against Mortensen.

“After a thorough investigation by Draper Police, the City Prosecutor has determined that there is insufficient corroborating evidence to support filing criminal charges against either party,” the city said in the statement.
Paul and Mortensen have filed protective orders against each other in effective until a court hearing on April 30.

title: Sheinelle Jones In 'Tears Full of Gratitude' As She Marks 1st Birthday Since Husband's Death
summary: Sheinelle Jones rang in her 48th birthday on April 19 by thanking fans and loved ones for helping her "feel the sun" again for the first time since the death of her her husband, Uche Ojeh
story: Sheinelle Jones rang in her 48th birthday on April 19 by thanking fans and loved ones for helping her "feel the sun" again for the first time since the death of her her husband, Uche Ojeh.
The TODAY with Jenna and Sheinelle co-host shared images from her recent promo tour for her debut book, “Through Mom’s Eyes," in an April 19 Instagram post.
She opened up in a heartfelt caption about the love and support she's received over the last painful year since losing Uche at age 45 to brain cancer in May 2025.
"As I literally sit here — in tears — on an airplane headed home, I think it’s the perfect moment to check in with you. Today is my 48th birthday, and to be honest — I can’t stop crying.

"Thankful tears, painful tears because it’s my first without Uche, and tears full of gratitude reflecting on the last 7 days," she wrote, referring to the April 14 release of her book, which collects stories and insights from Sheinelle's interviews with the moms of some of today's luminaries across culture, including Lady Gaga, Lin-Manuel Miranda, Steph Curry, Matthew McConaughey and Venus and Serena Williams.

"I have never felt so much love in my life. Thank you for the prayers and support over the last year," Sheinelle continued. "I don’t have the words to express how it feels to have SOLD OUT crowds in every city … and what it feels like to take these group photos with your smiling faces."
She concluded by referencing one of the The Beatles' songs about spiritual renewal, the George Harrison-penned "Here Comes the Sun."
"Uche loved The Beatles; as I mark the beginning of '48' — I do .. feel the sun. Thank you so so much," she wrote.
Sheinelle's personal message comes days after Jenna Bush Hager presented her with a pink birthday cake topped with the cover image of her book live on the air on April 17.
“I’m not going to get emotional, but 47 was a lot,” said Sheinelle after Jenna sang happy birthday to her.

“When I turned 47, it was a tough day, my 47th birthday,” she said, referencing the difficult final weeks she spent with Uche and their three children: son Kayin, 16, and 13-year-old twins, Clara and Uche.
“I just couldn’t see the light at the end of the tunnel,” Sheinelle added.

“So, for 48 to be so wonderful and to be here with you,” she continued, placing her hand on Jenna’s hand, “and to be here with you guys, it lets you know that if you’re in something (dark), there is going to be light.”

title: Dad Takes Notes on All 130 Artists at Coachella Before Going with His Family. How the Trip Went
summary: Michael Brand took notes on all the artists at Coachella. He tells TODAY.com how the weekend with his wife and daughters went.
story: One California dad decided to make a curriculum for his family's trip to Coachella.
Michael Brand’s daughter, Ava Brand, posted a video of his elaborate Coachella preparation ritual, resulting in a viral moment for the father-daughter duo.

In the video, which has racked up over 3 million views, Michael, 68, writes down notes for Coachella acts in a packed notebook. Meanwhile, music blasts from his phone. His daughter zooms in on the list of artists. “There’s no notes for Justin Bieber,” she says.
“Well, it’s Justin Bieber,” he says — no explanation needed.
The video even caught the attention of Joe Jonas. “I’m hosting a party — does he wanna come?” Jonas commented.
The whole adventure began when Michael and his wife, Diane Brand, decided to take their daughters — Ava, Claire and Olivia — to the annual music festival for Claire’s 30th birthday.
The Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival happens annually across two weekends in Indio, California, about two hours away from the Brands’ home.
“I think my dad’s always wanted to go,” say Ava, who has attended the festival with her sisters.
For Michael, this was a way to connect with his two older daughters, who live in New York. “We don’t get to see them very often and our family trips are great experiences,” he says.
Compared to their last family trip for a wedding in New Jersey, this required some extra preparation for Michael. He decided to tune in to music from all the acts booked — over 130.
He came across a Coachella playlist featuring music from all the artists. And from there, he started listening on his morning walk.
“The initial people that caught my eye that I knew immediately: David Byrne, Iggy Pop, The Strokes and CMAT,” Michael says. “But part of the exercise was to learn about music that I don’t know.”
He invented a system for taking notes on each artist and remembering who he wanted to see.

“My intention was purely to say whether it was somebody that I would be interested in going to or not, not rating anybody, not ranking anybody, nothing like that,” he says. “So a lot of my notes are purely a yes or no or a plus or minus.”
Michael says he ended up with a list that was “way too long and very ambitious.”
Once at the festival, he says he especially enjoyed Joyce Manor, Teddy Swims, Iggy Pop and The XX at the top of his list. He was also amazed by Sabrina Carpenter’s show.
But the highlight for the family was getting to meet Joe Jonas, who was serious about his invitation in the TikTok comments.
“Aperol invited us to their little day club activation thing on Friday and the woman who was emailing me was like, ‘OK, come at 4:50 on Friday, we have a fun surprise,’’’ Ava shares.
The surprise was Jonas’ party.
“I was just purely shocked,” Ava says. “We got served a spritz from Joe Jonas, which was just really fun.
Michael describes his very first Coachella experience as one to remember.

“It was a great day. There’s a lot of walking. The key part of the outfit for my outfit was my shoes,” he says. “The whole vibe is fantastic. It’s very, very well organized, runs pretty much on time and nothing but good things to say about that.”
For Michael, the experience was “absolutely” worth it and so was studying the music beforehand. He’s already thinking of Coachella next year.
“I’ve run a marathon or two and it’s like running a marathon,” Michael says. “The deal is you don’t say you’re going to do it or not. You can give yourself some time to think about it.”

title: The 2026 Citi Concert Series Lineup Is Here! See Who Will Hit the Plaza This Summer
summary: 2026 Citi Concert Series lineup on TODAY includes KATSEYE, Melanie Martinez, Megan Moroney, Zara Larsson, Mumford & Sons. Shaboozey and more artists.
story: It’s a sure sign that summer is almost here — the Citi Concert Series on TODAY is back for 2026!

Guests who do not obtain a Fan Pass can still attend the general admission line the morning of the concert, and those fans will be let in if space allows. Other answers to commonly asked questions about the series can be found here.

title: Trump may send Afghan allies who were promised new lives in the U.S. to Congo instead
summary: Hundreds of Afghan refugees who helped the United States fight the Taliban may be sent to the Democratic Republic of Congo after the Trump administration halted their U.S. resettlement, an advocacy group said
story: Hundreds of Afghan refugees who helped the United States fight the Taliban may be sent to the Democratic Republic of Congo after the Trump administration halted their U.S. resettlement, an advocacy group said.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

Shawn VanDiver, president of San Diego-based advocacy group AfghanEvac, said he had been briefed on the DRC plan by multiple officials with direct knowledge of it who work either at the State Department or in close coordination with it.
The Trump administration plan was first reported by The New York Times.

The Democratic Republic of Congo, a Central African country where the Afghans have no ties, is experiencing one of the world’s worst humanitarian crises, with more than 600,000 refugees there already after decades of armed conflict.
VanDiver accused the Trump administration of intentionally offering the Afghan refugees an alternative so bad that they would choose to return to Afghanistan even if it means risking their lives.
“This is insane,” he told NBC News in an interview, noting in a separate statement that “you do not solve the world’s number one refugee crisis by dumping it into the world’s number two.”
The Democratic Republic of Congo government and its U.S. embassy did not immediately respond to requests for comment overnight.

A State Department spokesperson said the U.S. is continuing "to work to identify options for voluntary resettlement of all Camp As Sayliyah (CAS) residents,” and they remain in regular and direct communication with residents on resettlement efforts.

The spokesperson also described moving the residents to a third country as "a positive resolution that provides safety for these remaining people to start a new life outside of Afghanistan while upholding the safety and security of the American people."
The State Department declined to provide any further information regarding the ongoing negotiations citing the sensitivity of the discussions.

Advocates for Afghan refugees at the camp, which is slated for closure, say the Trump administration is trying to pressure them into returning to Taliban-ruled Afghanistan, where they potentially face persecution, imprisonment or death under the Islamic fundamentalist regime.

There are about 1,100 Afghans at Camp As Sayliyah, a former U.S. military base outside Doha, where they were evacuated to await U.S. resettlement after U.S.-led troops withdrew from Afghanistan in 2021. They include former members of the Afghan special forces, interpreters who worked with the U.S. military and others whose work puts at them risk of persecution by the Taliban.

Most of the people at the camp have been approved for U.S. settlement after extensive security screening, and more than 400 of them are children. Many have been waiting for months or years to be reunited with family in the U.S., including relatives of U.S. service members and veterans.
The State Department said earlier this year that it planned to empty the camp by March 31, a deadline that came and went with no updates.

The DRC is unable to support the refugees it already has, most of whom are from neighboring Rwanda and the Central African Republic, VanDiver said. It’s also unclear what protections if any the Afghans would have against repatriation.
“There’s no jobs. They’re in the middle of a civil war. It’s not a place for Afghans,” he said. “They’re just going to end up getting deported back to Afghanistan by the DRC government.”

A senior executive at a U.S.-based resettlement group that works with Afghan refugees told NBC News they were also briefed on the DRC plan by one of the same State Department officials, as well as multiple others working at the department or in close coordination with it. The executive declined to be publicly identified in order to protect those relationships.

Choosing between the DRC and Afghanistan means “you go forward into a country that also has war, instability, or you go back where it is sure that you will face persecution from your previous government,” the executive said. “It is a false choice for these incredibly vulnerable people who deserve better.”
Afghan refugees in Qatar protest over travel suspension

As part of his immigration crackdown, Trump has blocked virtually all paths to the U.S. for Afghan allies, more than 190,000 of whom were resettled in the U.S. from August 2021 until mid-2025. Afghan allies and their family members have been detained by U.S. immigration officials, with one of them dying last month after less than 24 hours in custody. (Immigration and Customs Enforcement said the death of Mohommad Nazeer Paktyawal, 41, was being investigated.)
Restrictions were further tightened after a November shooting in Washington killed one National Guard member and seriously injured another.

The suspect, identified as Rahmanullah Lakanwal, 29, is an Afghan national who served alongside U.S. troops as part of an elite CIA-backed unit in Afghanistan. He was granted asylum by the Trump administration last year after having arrived in the U.S. during the Biden administration.
Retribution from the Taliban is not the only challenge facing refugees who return to Afghanistan, where the U.S. war began in 2001.
Women’s rights have been severely curtailed since the Taliban’s return to power, and the country is facing a number of humanitarian crises, including widespread malnutrition.
Afghanistan has also been engaged in a deadly conflict with neighboring Pakistan, with Pakistani airstrikes killing civilians in Kabul and elsewhere.

VanDiver said the Trump administration has been in negotiations with dozens of countries, many of them in Africa, to accept the Afghans currently in Qatar. He said the talks were likely to have been complicated by other actions by the administration, which has included many of the same countries in travel bans or required their citizens to pay thousands of dollars in visa bonds before traveling to the U.S.

Those negotiations are separate from the ones the Trump administration has been holding with various countries, including the Democratic Republic of Congo, to accept migrants from other countries who face deportation from the U.S. In exchange, the U.S. is paying millions of dollars to their governments, some of which have records of human rights abuses.
Critics of the Trump administration’s treatment of Afghan allies say it could harm U.S. national security by making local populations less willing to work with U.S. forces in future conflicts.
VanDiver noted that there is nothing stopping the Trump administration from bringing the hundreds of Afghans at the camp who have passed security screening to the U.S. as promised.
In the meantime, he said, the continuing uncertainty of the situation has taken a severe toll on their mental well-being.
“They’re reaching their breaking point,” he said.

title: Robot breaks the human half-marathon world record, a year after humanoid humiliation
summary: It was not even close as a bright-red Chinese humanoid named “Lightning” lived up to its name in a half-marathon pitting humans against robots, smoking its competition on Sunday.
story: BEIJING — It was not even close as a bright-red Chinese humanoid named “Lightning” lived up to its name in a half-marathon pitting humans against robots, smoking its competition on Sunday.
Subscribe to read this story ad-free
Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content.

The bipedal humanoid, developed by Chinese smartphone maker Honor, blazed through the 13-mile race in just 50 minutes, 26 seconds, besting all 12,000 human competitors and even surpassing the human world record for a half-marathon, set by Ugandan long-distance runner Jacob Kiplimo in Lisbon last month, by nearly seven minutes.
Lightning was slowed down only slightly when it crashed into a railing near the end of the race, with the machine helped back up, recovering to make a dramatic finish.
It was a clean sweep for Honor’s robots, which also took second and third place medals on the podium.

“I felt very nervous,” said Ma Huaze, captain of one of the winning Honor teams. “The biggest challenge was having the courage to perform and test large-scale upgrades on a major competitive stage like this.”

The event in an industrial park area of Beijing featured more than a hundred robots, a significant increase from last year’s inaugural race, when several of the 21 participating humanoids stumbled, careened out of control or just laid down at the starting line, and only six managed to make it across the finish line.
The fastest robot last year, an imposing machine named Tiangong, took 2 hours and 40 minutes to complete the half-marathon, well below the speed of a human pro athlete.

There was no repeat of that humiliation this year, with China’s technological advances on full display: At least four of the humanoids had sub-one-hour times, and there were far fewer units fritzing out. The real-life runners — who were in a parallel lane to avoid any human-on-robot collisions — were impressed by the speed of machines, some slowing down or stopping to take photos of their metallic competitors.
The fastest human to compete, 29-year-old Zhao Haijie, who ran the race in 1 hour, 7 minutes and 47 seconds, said the robots were really on the move within the first 3 miles.
“I felt it was going quite fast,” Zhao said in an interview with NBC News after the race. “It just went whoosh right past me.”

Nearly 40% of the robots were autonomous and running the full distance on their own as they navigated turns, uneven terrain and other obstacles. Other competitors were operated by remote control, with finishing times adjusted to reflect the different categories.
“I can feel the rapid advancements in our country’s technology,” said Liu Yanli, a 41-year-old financial worker watching from the sidelines with his family.
“For those of us in middle age, it gives us a great sense of security for the future,” he said. Liu’s 6-year-old son, Jinyu, said he liked the robots “carrying a lot of things on them.”

The swarm of high-speed machines looked a bit like science fiction, yet behind the spectacle is a higher-stakes competition between China and the U.S. to develop next-generation humanoids powered by artificial intelligence.

For Xue Qingheng, the race was a public stress test of changes his startup company, Intercity Technology Co., has made to its humanoid model over the past year. The child-size robot named Xiao Cheng has been outfitted with improved motor speed and coordination, as well as better feet, sensors and gait algorithm.

“For us, this process is really about competing against who we were last year,” Xue said in an interview at the company’s studio in Beijing before the event. “This year, we are only racing against ourselves.”

The race champion is set to receive orders worth over a million yuan, or $146,500, according to city officials organizing the event, as well as capturing attention in a country that has more than 150 humanoid-making companies and research labs. Hundreds of millions of people watched livestream coverage of the race across various platforms.

An intense focus on robotics and AI in China is no accident, with the state providing subsidies for development as well as demand in a top-down strategy to dominate an industry it regards as a “national priority.”

China already dominates the supply chain for AI chips, sensors and batteries, while Beijing’s latest master plan for 2026-2030 sets out a blueprint for futuristic technologies, including brain chips, quantum computing and factories manned by robots that look and move like people.
